title: The protein phosphatase inhibitor okadaic acid inhibits exit from metaphase II in parthenogenetically activated pig oocytes
abstract:
THE JOURNAL OF EXPERIMENTAL ZOOLOGY 277:49–56 (1997)
© 1997 WILEY-LISS, INC.
JEZ 719
The Protein Phosphatase Inhibitor Okadaic Acid
Inhibits Exit From Metaphase II in
Parthenogenetically Activated Pig Oocytes
RENATA GROCHOLOVÁ,1 JAROSLAV PETR,2,* JIR
v Í ROZINEK,3 AND
FRANTIS
vEK JÍLEK3
1Sanatorium Pronatal, IVF Unit, Praha-Stodu
οlky, 147 00 Czech Republic
2Research Institute of Animal Production, 104 00 Praha 10-Uhr
víne
vves,
Czech Republic
3Czech Agriculture University, Department of Veterinary Sciences,
Praha 6-Suchdol, 165 21 Czech Republic
ABSTRACT
The exposure of in vitro matured pig oocytes to the calcium ionophore A 23187 (50
µM, 7 min) resulted in parthenogenetic activation in 67% of the oocytes. When the activated oo-
cytes were cultured, they formed pronuclei. In these oocytes, tubulin labelling revealed a rear-
rangement of the microtubules into an interphase meshwork. The activated oocytes also lost their
ability to form cytoplasmic asters after short-term taxol treatment. The activation rate of the
oocytes was further increased when they were cultured with a protein synthesis inhibitor, cyclo-
heximide, after ionophore treatment. A culture of ionophore-treated oocytes with okadaic acid, the
inhibitor of protein phosphatases 1 and 2A, prevents the events characterizing oocyte activation.
In oocytes cultured with okadaic acid, chromatin remained condensed, and cytoplasm retained its
ability to respond to taxol treatment by the formation of cytoplasmic asters. This effect of okadaic
acid was observed even in oocytes in which the activating stimulus was followed by a culture with
cycloheximide. This data allows us to conclude that protein phosphatases 1 and 2A play an impor-
tant role during the transition from metaphase II to interphase after activation of the pig oocyte.
J. Exp. Zool. 277:49–56, 1997.

Contract Grant sponsor: GA C

*Correspondence to: Jaroslav Petr, Institute of Animal Production,
104 00 Praha 10-Uhr
víne
vves, Czech Republic.
Received 3 May 1996; Revision accepted 20 August 1996
Activation of oocytes represents a very important
technique now used in embryo cloning. For embryo
cloning, the nuclei of embryonic cells are transferred
to the cytoplasm of recipient cytoplast prepared by
enucleation of matured oocytes. The cell cycle be-
gins in such reconstructed embryos only when the
cytoplasm of recipient cytoplast is activated. That
is why much attention is focused on the activation
of oocytes in large domestic mammals.
The mechanisms regulating the activation of
oocytes and the transition from metaphase II to
interphase of the first embryonic cell cycle are not
yet fully understood. The maturation of mamma-
lian oocytes is completed at the metaphase II
stage, when the first polar body is extruded and
the chromosomes are arranged into a metaphase
plate. Meiosis is arrested at this stage (Hashimoto
and Kishimoto, ’88). This metaphase II arrest is
due to activity of the cytostatic factor (CSF), which
stabilizes the M-phase promoting factor (MPF)
and so retains high MPF activity (Masui, ’91;
Masui and Markert, ’71). Further progress of
meiosis and its completion depends on the activa-
tion stimulus, which is introduced with the sperm
during fertilization but can also be mimicked by
a broad scale of physical or chemical stimuli
(Yanagimachi, ’94). The activity of CSF disappears
during activation. This makes possible the inacti-
vation of MPF and the exit of oocytes from
metaphase II (Masui, ’91).
Parthenogenetic activation can be induced in pig
using electrical pulses (Prather et al., ’91; Procházka
et al., ’92; Rickords et al., ’93), ethanol (Didion et
al., ’90), or calcium ionophore (Funahashi et al.,
’94; Hagen et al., ’91; Saito et al., ’93), and differ-
ent protocols yield very variable results. Experi-
ments dealing with the activation of bovine
oocytes demonstrated that the effect of the acti-
vating stimuli can be enhanced after culture of
the activated oocytes with inhibitors of protein
synthesis (Shi et al., ’93; Yang et al., ’94). A simi-
lar situation was observed in activated mouse
 50
R. GROCHOLOVÁ ET AL.
(Bos-mikich et al., ’95; Hagemann et al., ’95) and
pig oocytes (Nussbaum and Prather, ’95).
Just as the majority of cell cycle events involved
in the transition from metaphase II to interphase
after the activation of oocytes, these processes are
also regulated by the cascade of phosphorylation
and dephosphorylation events (for review see
Motlík and Kubelka, ’90; Parrish et al., ’92). It
was shown in rodents that inhibition of protein
kinases enhances oocyte activation (Moses and
Masui, ’94). On the other hand, activation of pro-
tein phosphates is necessary for the activation of
rat oocytes (Zernicka-Goetz et al., ’93).
The aim of this study was to investigate changes
in chromatin configuration and microtubule ar-
rangement during activation of in vitro matured
pig oocytes. The ability of oocytes to assemble tu-
bulin was assessed after exposure of the oocytes
to taxol, a drug that promotes microtubule assem-
bly by shifting the equilibrium between the tubu-
lin polymer and dimer in favour of microtubule
formation (Horwitz, ’92; Schiff et al., ’79). The in-
fluence of protein synthesis on the activation of
oocytes was investigated using the protein syn-
thesis inhibitor cycloheximide. Further, we tested
the hypothesis that inhibition of protein phos-
phatases prevents activation of pig oocytes. We
used for this purpose okadaic acid as the specific
inhibitor of protein phosphatases 1 and 2A (Bialojan
and Takai, ’88; Cohen et al., ’90).
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Oocyte isolation, culture, and activation
Pig ovaries were obtained from a local slaugh-
terhouse from gifts at an unknown stage of the
oestrous cycle. Ovaries were transported to the labo-
ratory in a saline solution (0.9% NaCl) warmed to
39°C. Oocytes were aspirated from follicles of a
diameter from 2–5 mm using a 20 gauge needle.
Only oocytes with compact cumulus were used for
the culture.
Oocytes were washed three times in a culture
medium: M199 medium with Earle’s salts (ÚSOL,
Prague, Czech Republic) supplemented with 0.039
ml of 7.5% natrium carbonate per millilitre of me-
dium, calcium lactate (0.6 mg/ml of medium),
gentamycin (0.025 mg/ml), and 10% of bovine se-
rum (ZD Hustopec
ve, Czech Republic).
In vitro matured oocytes were obtained after a
48 h culture in 30 mm petri dishes (Nunc, Roskilde,
Denmark) in 3 ml of culture medium at a tem-
perature of 39°C in a mixture of air with 5% CO2.
At the end of the culture, a part of the oocytes was
mounted on slides, fixed in acetalkohol (1:3, v/v)
for at least 24 h, and stained with 1% orcein to
check meiotic maturation in the cultured oocytes.
The oocytes were observed under a phase contrast
microscope to check nuclear maturation.
In vitro matured oocytes were activated after a
7 min exposure to 50 µM of calcium ionophore A
23187 (Sigma, St. Louis, MO) in the above de-
scribed culture medium M199 not supplemented
with bovine serum. The oocytes were carefully
washed after the ionophore treatment and sub-
jected to further treatment.
To test the role of protein synthesis in activa-
tion, the ionophore-treated oocytes were cultured
for 24 h in a culture medium M199 with cyclo-
heximide (10 µg/ml).
The role of protein phosphatases was tested by
a 24 h culture of ionophore-treated oocytes in a
culture medium M199 with 1 µM okadaic acid.
Reversibility of the cycloheximide effects by
okadaic acid was tested by a 24 h culture of iono-
phore-treated oocytes in a culture medium M199
supplemented with both cycloheximide (10 µg/ml)
and okadaic acid (1 µM).
In control experiments, the in vitro matured pig
oocytes were not exposed to calcium ionophore but
were cultured for 24 h either in culture medium
M199 or culture medium M199 supplemented ei-
ther with cycloheximide (10 µg/ml) or with okadaic
acid (1 µM).
At the end of culture the oocytes were processed
for immunocytochemistry for tubulin and stained
with Hoechst 33258 for chromatin observation.
Immunocytochemistry for tubulin
and Hoechst staining
Zona-free oocytes were washed in a HEPES
buffer and then fixed for 60 min at room tempera-
ture in a HEPES buffer containing 2.5% formal-
dehyde, 2.5 mM MgCl, 2.5 mM EGTA, and 1 µM
taxol. After careful washing in the phosphate buff-
ered saline (PBS) buffer with 0.1% of Triton X and
0.1% human serum albumin (HSA), the oocytes
were incubated together with a mouse monoclonal
antibody to alpha-tubulin (Sigma), diluted in PBS
supplemented with 0.1% HSA and 0.01% Tween
20 (antibody dilution 1:200) for 13–16 h at 4°C.
Following three washes in PBS, the oocytes were
incubated for 1 h with a ovine antibody to mouse
IgG (Sigma) conjugated with FITC diluted in PBS
supplemented with 0.1% HSA and 0.01% Tween
20 (antibody dilution 1:50). After careful washing,
the oocytes were stained with Hoechst 33 258 (10
µg/ml PBS buffer) for 10 min at room tempera-
ture. After washing in a PBS buffer with 0.1%

µM, 7 min) resulted in parthenogenetic activation in 67% of the oocytes. When the activated oo-
cytes were cultured, they formed pronuclei. In these oocytes, tubulin labelling revealed a rear-

with poly-L-lysine and covered with a cover glass
in glycerol with Tris buffer, pH 8.5 (1:1 v/v).

To evaluate the nucleating ability of cytoplas-
mic microtubule, oocytes were incubated in a cul-

The majority of pig oocytes cultured in vitro for
48 h reached metaphase II stage (111/120, or 93%),
and none of these oocytes became spontaneously
activated. Further culture of matured oocytes for
an additional 24 h resulted only rarely in sponta-
neous parthenogenetic activation (1/120, or 1%)
(Table 1). When the matured oocytes at metaphase
II stage were stained with Hoechst, we observed
oocyte chromosomes arranged in a metaphase
plate (Fig. 1A). Tubulin labelling revealed a
metaphase II spindle in these oocytes. A short ex-
posure of these oocytes to taxol induced the for-
mation of numerous cytoplasmic asters (Fig. 1B).
In vitro matured oocytes were exposed to 50 µM
calcium ionophore A 23187 and then cultured for
24 h in a culture medium. Approximately two-
thirds of the oocytes (81/120, or 67%) exhibited
activation. We observed chromatin decondensation
and pronuclei formation in activated oocytes
stained with Hoechst. The tubulin labelling re-
vealed that the microtubule rearranged into an
interphase meshwork and culture with taxol did
not induce the formation of cytoplasmic asters
(Fig. 2B). The percentage of activated oocytes was
further increased when matured oocytes, activated
using a calcium ionophore, were cultured with a
protein synthesis inhibitor, cycloheximide. The
microtubule arrangement and chromosome con-
figuration in cycloheximide-treated, activated oo-
cytes did not differ from those observed in activated
oocytes cultured in a cycloheximide-free medium
(Fig. 3A,B). Matured oocytes exposed to cyclohex-
imide without an activating stimulus did not ex-
hibit a significant activation rate (activation rate
When in vitro matured oocytes were exposed to
a calcium ionophore and then cultured with 1 µM
okadaic acid, only 3% (4/120) of them became ac-
tivated. Chromatin in these oocytes was either
condensed into a clump or the chromosomes were
dispersed within a cytoplasm (Fig. 4A). The ab-
sence of a meiotic spindle was noted after tubulin
labelling. However, oocytes retained their ability
to respond to taxol treatment by the formation of
cytoplasmic asters (Fig. 4B). The okadaic acid pre-
vents transition to the interphase even in oocytes
exposed to calcium ionophore and then cultured
with cycloheximide (activation rate 10%, or 12/
120). The chromatin in these oocytes was also con-
densed, and labelling for tubulin revealed the ab-
sence of a meiotic spindle. The cytoplasm of the
oocytes retained their response to taxol treatment
and formed the cytoplasmic asters after short-
term taxol treatment (Fig. 5A,B).
To distinguish the effects of the activating
stimulus and okadaic acid upon the configuration
of the chromatin and microtubule arrangement,
the matured oocytes at metaphase II stage were
cultured with 1 µM okadaic acid. These oocytes
exhibited condensed chromatin and lacked the

meiotic spindle, and the asters were slightly
formed in their cytoplasm in response to taxol
In this study we proved that the active role of
protein phosphatases is necessary for full activa-
tion and transition from metaphase II to the in-
terphase in pig oocytes. In our study the pig
oocytes were activated using a calcium ionophore
similarly to the experiments performed by Hagen
et al. (’91), Saito et al. ’93), and Funahashi et al.
(’94). Calcium ionophore A 23187 is known to in-
crease the intracellular concentrations of calcium
(Reed, ’82). Elevated levels of calcium in oocyte
cytoplasm can induce MPF inactivation as it shifts
the equilibrium between the synthesis and de-
struction of one of the MPF components—cyclin—
toward increased destruction (Lorca et al., ’91).
Elevated cytoplasmic calcium concentrations can
also play a part in MPF inactivation through the
inactivation of CSF which protects cyclin from deg-
radation (Murray and Kirschner, ’89). The impor-
tance of decreased protein synthesis for transit to
interphase after oocyte activation was shown in
rodents (Hagemann et al., ’95; Zernicka-Goetz, ’91)
and also in cattle (Shi et al., ’93; Yang et al., ’94)
and pig (Nussbaum and Prather, ’95). A similar
effect was also observed in our study. Calcium
ionophore–treated oocytes exhibited a significantly
higher activation rate after their exposure to cyclo-
heximide. It is assumed that the positive effect of
protein synthesis inhibitors on the transition of ac-
tivated oocytes to the interphase is due to the ad-
ditional suppression of protein synthesis, which
was not satisfactorily suppressed by the activat-
ing stimulus alone (Yang et al., ’94).
The transition from G2 to M-phase during the
meiotic maturation of oocytes is characterised by
chromosome condensation, nuclear membrane
breakdown, and the formation of a meiotic spindle.
All these processes are regulated by p34cdc2 pro-
tein kinase, which forms a catalytic subunit of
MPF (Motlík and Kubelka, ’90; Parrish et al., ’92).
Similarly, an important role is played by the cas-
cade of dephosphorylation events during the exit
from metaphase. Rat oocytes cannot be activated
after exposure to the protein synthesis inhibitor
puromycin when they are exposed to okadaic acid
at the same time (Zernicka-Goetz and Maro, ’93).
In our study the inhibitor of protein phosphatases
1 and 2A blocked the transition to interphase in
matured pig oocytes exposed to calcium ionophore.
Okadaic acid prevented transition to the inter-
phase in oocytes which were exposed to the pro-
tein synthesis inhibitor cycloheximide after the
activation stimulus performed by calcium iono-
phore. Hence, the effect of okadaic acid cannot be
attributed to insufficiency of the activating stimu-
lus. The chromatin of oocytes remained condensed
when they were exposed to calcium ionophore and
then cultured with okadaic acid. Cytoplasmic as-
ters were formed in the cytoplasm of these oo-
cytes after their short-term taxol treatment. It
suggests that the high competence of cytoplasm
for tubulin assembly, which is typical for the cy-
toplasm of M-phase oocytes, was maintained
(Albertini, ’92; Messinger and Albertini, ’92; Van
Cauweberge and Alexandre, ’92). Hence, okadaic
acid prevents the rearrangement of microtubules
into a meshwork, which usually occurs after exit
after metaphase II after fertilization or parthe-
nogenetic activation (Albertini, ’92; Maro et al.,
’90; Navara et al., ’94; Yllera-Fernandez et al., ’92).
Chromatin in activated oocytes cultured with
okadaic acid was observed to form a clump or to
be dispersed within a cytoplasm. This chromatin
configuration is due to the absence of a meiotic
spindle. The lack of a meiotic spindle after okadaic
acid treatment was also observed in activated rat
oocytes (Zernicka-Goetz et al., ’93). Since a mei-
otic spindle was not observed in okadaic acid–
treated oocytes which were not exposed to an
activating stimulus either, we can assume that
the disappearance of the meiotic spindle was not
caused by the activating stimulus but was due to
the side effects of okadaic acid. The negative ef-
fect of okadaic acid upon the meiotic spindle in
matured oocytes was also described in rodents (de
Pennart et al., ’93; Zernicka-Goetz and Maro, ’93).
Okadaic acid was described as a substance able
to induce the resumption of meiosis in oocytes of
numerous species (starfish: Picard et al., ’89; frog:
Rime et al., ’90; mouse: Gavin et al., ’91; pig and
cattle: Kalous et al., ’93), and the disruption of
the meiotic spindle or its absence was also de-
scribed in these cases. It was shown in metaphase
II meiotic spindles that some proteins associated
with a meiotic spindle are dephosphorylated in
the presence of okadaic acid. The dephosphory-
lated proteins had a lower affinity with microtu-
bules which became less stable (Zernicka-Goetz
and Maro, ’93). Similar effects of okadaic acid were
also observed in somatic cells (Ghosh et al., ’92;
Gurland and Gundersen, ’93; Vandré and Wills,
’92). Alexandre et al. (’91) described various ef-
fects of okadaic acid on tubulin assembly in mouse
oocytes. In pig oocytes, the tubulin assembly also
OKADAIC ACID BLOCKS OOCYTE ACTIVATION
seems to be impaired. After okadaic acid treat-
ment, the asters were only slightly formed in oo-
cyte cytoplasm in response to taxol treatment.
This effect was more obvious in unactivated than
in activated oocytes. The reason for the difference
From our results, we can conclude that okadaic
acid, as a potent inhibitor of protein phosphatases
1 and 2A, is able to block transition to the inter-
phase in matured pig oocytes after their activa-
tion. The absence of a meiotic spindle in oocytes
exposed to an activating stimulus and subse-
quently cultured with okadaic acid is due to the
effect of okadaic acid upon microtubules and not
to any microtubule rearrangement during transi-
tion from metaphase to interphase.
